question_text: The diagram shows Earth at different points in its orbit around the Sun. At position A, the Northern Hemisphere receives solar radiation at an angle of 30° to the surface. At position C (opposite side of orbit), calculate the angle of solar radiation to the surface in the Northern Hemisphere, and explain why this creates winter conditions.
correct_answer: 150° (or the Sun's rays hit at 30° on the other side, creating a shallower angle). When the angle is shallower (more obtuse/further from perpendicular), the same amount of solar energy is spread over a larger area, providing less energy per unit area. This causes lower temperatures and winter conditions.
explanation: When Earth is on the opposite side of its orbit, the Northern Hemisphere is tilted away from the Sun. The angle of incidence becomes shallower (approximately 150° measured from the surface normal, or 30° measured from the horizon). Shallower angles mean solar energy is spread over a larger surface area, reducing the energy concentration and causing winter. Additionally, days are shorter, providing even less total solar heating.
